questions.promptTex: \text{A box plot's box stretches from the } \_\_\_ \text{ to the } \_\_\_.
hints: The box represents the middle 50% — between the quartiles.
Q1 to Q3.
questions.explanation: The box spans Q1 to Q3 (the IQR — middle 50% of data).
steps: Box plot anatomy: box from **Q1 to Q3** (the IQR), with a line at the median inside the box.
Whiskers extend from the box out to the min and max (or to fences for outlier plots).

questions.promptTex: \text{A distribution is } \textbf{right-skewed}. \text{ Compare mean and median:}
hints: Right-skewed = long tail on the right. Mean is dragged to the right.
Mean > median in right-skewed data.
questions.explanation: Right-skewed: tail on the right pulls the mean above the median.
steps: Right-skew = long tail on the **right** (toward larger values).
Outliers in the tail drag the **mean** toward them.
Median is largely unaffected.
Result: **mean > median** in right-skewed distributions.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which is the BEST display to compare distributions of three different classes?}
hints: Box plots side-by-side make comparison super easy: at a glance you see medians, spreads, skew.
Pie charts don't show distributions of numerical data.
questions.explanation: Side-by-side box plots are designed for distribution comparison: medians, IQRs, ranges all directly comparable.
steps: Side-by-side box plots align all the key summary stats (median, quartiles, range) for instant comparison.
Histograms work too but are bulky — hard to compare 3 at once.
Pie charts are for categorical proportions, not distributions.
Scatterplots show two-variable relationships, not single-variable distributions.

questions.promptTex: \text{The bars in a histogram have NO gaps between them, but bars in a } \\ \text{bar chart DO. Why?}
choices: Histogram bins are continuous intervals; bar-chart categories are separate
Histograms use color, bar charts don't
hints: Histograms describe ranges of numerical data; bar charts describe distinct categories.
Continuous → no gap. Categorical → gaps.
questions.explanation: Histogram bins are adjacent intervals on a number line — gaps would imply missing values. Bar charts represent separate categories, so gaps emphasize separation.
steps: Histograms: x-axis is a continuous variable split into bins. Adjacent bins share endpoints, so bars touch.
Bar charts: x-axis lists discrete categories (no continuity). Gaps emphasize that they're separate.
**Continuous data → touching bars; categorical → gaps.**

hints: Symmetry → no skew → mean and median agree.
Both at the center.
questions.explanation: In a symmetric distribution, mean and median both sit at the center — they're approximately equal.
steps: Symmetric: equal weight on both sides of center.
No tail to drag the mean.
Mean ≈ median (and they're both at the center).

hints: Two peaks = two centers in the data.
Often the data combines two distinct populations.
questions.explanation: Two peaks frequently mean the data set is a mixture of two different groups (e.g., heights of adults of two species).
steps: A single peak (unimodal) is what we expect for a homogeneous population.
Two peaks (bimodal) often means the data secretly combines two subpopulations.
Real example: heights of all adults — bimodal peaks at the average male and average female heights.

questions.promptTex: \text{Stem-and-leaf plot: } \\ 3 | 4 7 \\ 4 | 1 5 8 \\ 5 | 2 6 \\ \text{How many values does this dataset have?}
hints: Each leaf is one value.
questions.explanation: Counting leaves: 34, 37, 41, 45, 48, 52, 56 — seven values.
steps: Each digit on the right side (a 'leaf') represents one data point.
Stem 3: leaves '4 7' → values 34, 37.
Stem 4: leaves '1 5 8' → values 41, 45, 48.
Stem 5: leaves '2 6' → values 52, 56.

questions.promptTex: \text{In a box plot, the median appears as:}
choices: The left edge of the box
The right edge of the box
hints: Median is the line dividing the box into two halves.
Inside, somewhere between Q1 and Q3.
questions.explanation: The median is the vertical line drawn inside the box (between Q1 and Q3).
steps: Box plot shows: min — Q1 [box: median line] Q3 — max.
The median is the **line inside the box**.
(Box edges are Q1 and Q3.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A histogram of test scores is highly LEFT-skewed. What does this suggest?}
choices: Most students did poorly; a few did very well
Most students did well; a few did very poorly
hints: Left-skewed: long tail on the LEFT (low values).
Bulk on the right (high scores), tail on the left (low scores).
questions.explanation: Left-skewed: long tail extends LEFT (toward low values), and the bulk (peak) sits on the RIGHT (high values).
steps: Left-skewed = long tail on the **left** (toward smaller values).
The 'bulk' of data sits on the **right**.
For test scores: most students scored high (bulk on right), a few scored very low (tail on left).
**Most did well; a few did poorly.**

questions.promptTex: \text{A box plot has a much longer right whisker than left. The data is:}
hints: Long whisker on the right means values stretch far to the right.
That's right-skew (tail on right).
questions.explanation: Long right whisker = data extends far to the right beyond Q3 = right-skewed.
steps: Whisker length on each side measures how far the extremes reach.
Long right whisker → max is far above Q3 → right tail is long.
**Right-skewed.**

questions.promptTex: \text{The five-number summary is } (4, 12, 18, 28, 80). \text{ This dataset is likely:}
hints: Distance from Q3 to max: $80 - 28 = 52$. From min to Q1: $12 - 4 = 8$.
Right side stretches MUCH more — right-skewed.
questions.explanation: Right whisker length $52$ vs left whisker length $8$ → strongly right-skewed.
steps: Compare whisker lengths.
Left whisker (min to Q1): $12 - 4 = 8$.
Right whisker (Q3 to max): $80 - 28 = 52$.
Right is much longer → **right-skewed**.
(Likely an outlier on the right.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Which display is BEST for showing every individual data point in a small dataset?}
hints: Dot plot shows one dot per data point — no aggregation.
Histograms and box plots aggregate.
questions.explanation: Dot plots show every individual point as a separate dot — best for small datasets where you want to see each value.
steps: Dot plot: one dot per value, stacked above the value's position. **Every point visible.**
Histogram: groups data into bins → individual points lost.
Box plot: only 5 summary numbers shown.
Pie chart: shows proportions, not values.

questions.prompt: A histogram of exam scores has these frequencies: bin 10-20 has 5 students, bin 20-30 has 12, bin 30-40 has 18, bin 40-50 has 9, bin 50-60 has 6. Which is the MODAL CLASS (the bin with the most data)?
hints: Find the bin with the highest frequency (tallest bar).
30-40 has 18 — the highest.
questions.explanation: The MODAL CLASS is the bin with the highest frequency (the tallest bar). With 18 students, 30-40 is the modal class. The mode itself for grouped data is typically taken as the midpoint of this class (≈ 35).
steps: **Recognize the type — reading a histogram.** The 'mode' for grouped data is the bin with the highest frequency, called the *modal class*.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 320 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><line x1="30" y1="170" x2="300" y2="170" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="30" y1="170" x2="30" y2="20" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><rect x="30" y="145" width="50" height="25" fill="#bfdbfe" stroke="#1e3a8a" stroke-width="1.2"/><rect x="80" y="110" width="50" height="60" fill="#bfdbfe" stroke="#1e3a8a" stroke-width="1.2"/><rect x="130" y="80" width="50" height="90" fill="#fca5a5" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="2"/><rect x="180" y="125" width="50" height="45" fill="#bfdbfe" stroke="#1e3a8a" stroke-width="1.2"/><rect x="230" y="140" width="50" height="30" fill="#bfdbfe" stroke="#1e3a8a" stroke-width="1.2"/><text x="55" y="185" font-size="10" fill="#475569" text-anchor="middle">10-20</text><text x="105" y="185" font-size="10" fill="#475569" text-anchor="middle">20-30</text><text x="155" y="185" font-size="10" font-weight="700" fill="#b91c1c" text-anchor="middle">30-40</text><text x="205" y="185" font-size="10" fill="#475569" text-anchor="middle">40-50</text><text x="255" y="185" font-size="10" fill="#475569" text-anchor="middle">50-60</text><text x="55" y="140" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">5</text><text x="105" y="105" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">12</text><text x="155" y="75" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#b91c1c" text-anchor="middle">18</text><text x="205" y="120" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">9</text><text x="255" y="135" font-size="10" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">6</text><text x="60" y="40" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">modal class = tallest bar = 30-40</text></svg>

steps: **Find the tallest bar.** Frequencies: 5, 12, 18, 9, 6. Maximum: 18 → bin 30-40.

**(Estimate the mode itself if needed.)** For grouped data, the *modal value* is typically reported as the midpoint of the modal class: $(30 + 40)/2 = 35$. (More precise formulas refine this using the neighbors' frequencies.)

questions.prompt: In a scatterplot, points cluster tightly around a line going from upper-left to lower-right. What does this indicate?
hints: Upper-left to lower-right means: as $x$ increases, $y$ decreases.
That's negative correlation. The tight clustering indicates a STRONG one.
questions.explanation: Going from upper-left to lower-right means $y$ DECREASES as $x$ increases — that's negative correlation. The tight clustering near a line says the relationship is strong (close to linear). Numerically, this would correspond to a Pearson correlation coefficient $r$ near $-1$.
steps: **Recognize the shape.** Direction (positive/negative) is the slope direction. Tightness (strong/weak) is how closely points hug the line.
**Upper-left → lower-right.** As $x$ moves right, $y$ moves down. Negative slope → negative correlation.
**Tight clustering.** Points right on the line ⇒ strong relationship.
**Numerical scale (Pearson $r$).** $r = +1$: perfect positive line. $r = 0$: no linear relationship. $r = -1$: perfect negative line. Tight upper-left → lower-right ≈ $r \approx -0.9$ or stronger.

**Pattern lesson — don't confuse correlation with causation.** $r$ describes association, NOT cause. Two correlated variables may share a common cause without one causing the other. Always interpret scatterplots in context.

questions.prompt: Which display is BEST for showing monthly average temperature in a city over 12 months?
hints: Time-series data wants to emphasize the trend over time.
Line graphs connect successive time points, making the seasonal cycle visible.
questions.explanation: *Time-series* data (measurements at successive points in time) is best shown by a line graph. The connecting lines emphasize the seasonal pattern. Pie charts are for proportions; histograms collapse the time axis; stem-and-leaf is for individual values without time order.
steps: **Recognize the data type — time series.** One variable measured at successive time points. The natural emphasis is the trend or cycle over time.
**Line graph.** Plot temperature on the y-axis vs month on the x-axis. Connect consecutive months. The line makes seasonal patterns (summer peak, winter trough) instantly visible.

**Why not the others?** Pie chart loses time order entirely. Histogram bins the temperature values, losing which month each came from. Stem-and-leaf lists values but doesn't convey time order well either.

**Pattern lesson — match display to story.** Time series → line. Comparing groups → side-by-side box plots or bar charts. Distribution shape → histogram. Two-variable relationship → scatterplot. Parts of a whole → pie chart or stacked bar.

questions.prompt: A bar chart of company quarterly sales has a y-axis starting at $90 million (not $0), making three bars at $92M, $95M, $98M look dramatically different. This chart is:
choices: Acceptable — y-axis scale is the author's choice
Misleading — the visual differences are exaggerated by the broken axis; relative size of bars suggests bigger differences than the data shows
hints: Visually, the bar at $98M would look ~3× taller than the bar at $92M with a y-axis starting at $90.
But the actual difference is only ~6% — a much smaller story than the visuals suggest.
questions.explanation: A 'broken axis' (one that doesn't start at zero) exaggerates visual differences. A bar at $92M vs $98M with y-axis at $90 looks like 1 unit vs 8 units — an 8× visual ratio for a 6% actual difference. Honest data displays start the y-axis at zero (or clearly label the truncation).
steps: **The trick — y-axis doesn't start at 0.** With y-axis starting at $90M, a bar at $92M has visible height '2', a bar at $98M has height '8'. The visual ratio is 4×.
**Compare to a zero-start axis.** Bar heights would be $92$ and $98$ — a $1.065×$ ratio. The story is 'modest 6% growth', not '4× growth'.

**When to flag.** Bar charts and column charts almost always want a zero-start y-axis. Line graphs sometimes use non-zero starts (when small relative changes matter), but always label the convention explicitly.
**Real-world consequences.** Manipulated y-axes have been used to overstate election results, drug-trial outcomes, and economic recoveries. Always check the y-axis range before believing the visual.
**Pattern lesson — be a skeptical reader.** Look at the axis numbers, not just the visual. Visualization can lie even when the underlying numbers are correct.

hints: Odd count → median is the 6th value = 34.
questions.explanation: Decoding the leaves: $21, 23, 25, 30, 32, 34, 37, 39, 41, 46, 48$ — eleven values (already sorted). Median = middle = 6th value = 34.
steps: **Decode the plot.** Each stem-leaf combination is one data value. Stem 2 + leaves 1, 3, 5 → values 21, 23, 25. Continuing: 30, 32, 34, 37, 39, 41, 46, 48.
**Count.** $3 + 5 + 3 = 11$ values total.
**Identify median position.** 11 values, sorted (stem-and-leaf is inherently sorted). Odd count → median is the *middle* value = the 6th.

**Why stem-and-leaf is great for medians.** Data is already sorted, individual values are preserved. You can read off the median, quartiles, range, and quickly spot duplicates — without any computation.

questions.prompt: A pie chart is most appropriate for displaying:
choices: Proportions of a single whole (e.g., percentage of a budget spent on each category)
hints: A pie shows slices summing to a whole — parts of one total.
Time series → line graph. Two variables → scatterplot. Distribution → histogram.
questions.explanation: Pie charts show parts of a single whole — slices that sum to 100%. They're appropriate when you have categorical proportions of one quantity (budget breakdown, market share, demographic percentages). They're poor for time series, distributions of continuous data, or comparing two variables.
steps: **When pie charts work.** Categorical data that adds up to a whole, with a small number of categories (≤ 6 or so) and meaningful proportional differences.

**When they fail.**

• Too many slices (>7) — hard to compare.
• Slices of similar size — hard to rank visually.
• Time series — pies don't show progression.
• Numerical (non-categorical) data — better suited to histograms or box plots.
• Comparing multiple wholes — better as stacked or grouped bar charts.

**Modern criticism.** Many data-viz experts (e.g., Edward Tufte) actively discourage pie charts: humans compare angles and areas worse than they compare lengths. A bar chart of the same data is usually more readable. But for small, well-known proportions (a budget breakdown), pies remain widely used for cultural familiarity.
**Pattern lesson — match display to question.** 'How is this whole broken up?' → pie. 'How has it changed?' → line. 'How is it distributed?' → histogram. Different questions need different charts.

questions.prompt: An OGIVE (cumulative frequency curve) is best used to:
choices: Show three distributions side by side
Read off percentiles, quartiles, or 'how many data points fall below value X'
Plot two variables against each other
hints: An ogive shows cumulative frequency vs value — always increasing.
At any value $x$, the height = how many points are ≤ $x$. So you can read off percentiles.
questions.explanation: An ogive plots cumulative frequency against the variable, producing an upward-rising curve from 0% to 100%. At any point on the curve, the height gives the fraction (or count) of data at or below that value. Pull horizontally at 50% to read off the median; at 25% → Q1; at 90% → 90th percentile.
steps: **What an ogive shows.** x-axis: variable value. y-axis: cumulative frequency (or relative cumulative percentage). The curve always rises monotonically from $0$ to $n$ (or 0% to 100%).
**How to read it.** Pick a percentile on the y-axis → draw a horizontal line → drop down to the x-axis → that's the value at that percentile.
**Reverse direction.** Pick a value on the x-axis → go up to the curve → over to the y-axis → that fraction of data is at or below the chosen value.
**When to use.** Quick visual reading of percentiles. Great for class grade thresholds (e.g., 'top 10% of scores are above what value?').
**Pattern lesson.** Ogive is to percentiles what a histogram is to frequencies. Same data, two complementary views.

questions.prompt: A survey of 200 people asks 'what is your favorite breakfast food?' (categorical responses: pancakes, eggs, cereal, oatmeal, smoothie). The BEST display is:
hints: Categorical data — distinct, unordered groups → BAR chart (with gaps).
Histograms are for NUMERICAL data binned into ranges.
questions.explanation: Categorical responses (no ordering, no arithmetic) call for a bar chart — one bar per category, with gaps between bars to emphasize discreteness. Histograms are for *numerical* data; the lack of gaps emphasizes continuity. Box plots and scatterplots both require numerical data.
steps: **Identify the data type.** Favorite breakfast = nominal categorical. No order, no arithmetic.
**Match the display.** Categorical → bar chart. Numerical (continuous) → histogram. Two numerical variables → scatterplot. One numerical variable's distribution → box plot or histogram.

**Why gaps matter.** A bar chart leaves gaps between bars because the categories are *separate things*, not adjacent ranges on a number line. A histogram has touching bars because the bins are adjacent intervals.
**A subtle improvement — Pareto chart.** A bar chart with bars sorted from tallest to shortest. Even faster scanning for 'most popular'. Same data, smarter order.
**Pattern lesson — first ask what the data is.** Numerical vs categorical, ordered vs unordered, one variable vs many. The right display follows from the answer.
